THOUGHT:
The existing citation (Hendrycks & Dietterich, 2019) effectively covers benchmarking robustness to synthetic corruptions, but it does not address the fundamental representational reasons *why* models fail when deployed in the wild. To satisfy the "Specific Concepts," "Highlighting Research Gaps," and "Supporting Arguments" categories, I need a foundational paper that explains how deep networks often rely on spurious correlations and dataset biases rather than learning robust, generalizable features. Geirhos et al.'s work on shortcut learning perfectly bridges this gap, providing cross-modal evidence of this pitfall and directly motivating the need for more rigorous real-world evaluation protocols. I will query Semantic Scholar to retrieve this paper.
